methods.description: Procedure for drilling an ice core, transporting it, and preparing samples for subsequent analyses, including cleaning, sectioning, melting, storage, and collection of organic matter for dating.

steps.description: In February 2003, a 22.53 m long core was drilled to the bedrock, using a 10-cm diameter drill, driven by a 220 V engine.
A total of 56 ice cores, ranging in length between 21 and 63 cm, were recovered and transported in frozen state to Stockholm University, Sweden.
steps.searchable_summary: recover and transport ice core sections

steps.description: In the cold laboratory, the ice core was cut lengthwise, half of it being archived.
steps.searchable_summary: cut ice core lengthwise and archive half
outputs: ice core halves (working half, archive half)

steps.description: The remaining half was cleaned by removing the outer layer of ice (0.5-1 cm thick), and subsequently cut in 1-2 cm wide samples that were allowed to melt at room temperature and stored in 20 ml high-density polyethylene flasks before analyses.
steps.searchable_summary: clean, section, melt, and store ice core samples

steps.description: Organic matter (leaf pieces and pine needles) was handpicked from the ice core for 14 C dating.
steps.searchable_summary: handpick organic matter from ice core for dating

methods.method_name: Ice Core Dating and Depth-Age Model Construction
methods.description: Methodology for dating ice core samples using 14C analysis, filtering results, and constructing a depth-age model using Bayesian software, incorporating historical information.

steps.description: A total of 35 samples were submitted for 14 C dating to the Poznan Radiocarbon Laboratory (Poland).

Of the resulting 29 determined ages, three were obviously contaminated (two with young organic matter, and one with old carbon, possibly by incorporating "infinite" aged carbon from the cave's host-rock).
steps.searchable_summary: identify and exclude contaminated radiocarbon ages

steps.description: The SIC core chronology (Fig. 2d) was constructed using the Bayesian software Bacon, which assumes that accumulation rate is always positive and can vary gradually from depth to depth.
steps.searchable_summary: construct ice core depth-age model using Bayesian software

steps.description: Historical observations have shown that between AD 1863 and 1982, enhanced melting and related changes in the geometry of the ice block led to the loss of ~100 cm of ice.
steps.searchable_summary: incorporate historical ice loss information into age model

steps.description: Based on annual ice accumulation rates derived independently of the current one (between 0.9 and 1.6 cm/year, with a mean value of ~1.3 cm/yr), we estimated an age of AD 1860 ± 20 (similar to 90 cal BP) for the top of the ice core.
steps.searchable_summary: estimate age of ice core top based on accumulation rates

methods.description: Measurement of stable isotopes (δ18O and δ2H) in melted ice core samples using Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry and reporting results on a standard scale.

steps.description: Stable isotope analyses were performed at the School of Geosciences, University of South Florida, using the equilibration method on a Thermo Delta V Advantage Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometer.
steps.searchable_summary: perform stable isotope analysis using IRMS
inputs: Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometer (Thermo Delta V Advantage)

steps.description: The results were normalized to the VSMOW-SLAP scale and reported in the conventional δ notation, in ‰ (per mil) against VSMOW.
steps.searchable_summary: normalize and report stable isotope results on VSMOW-SLAP scale
outputs: normalized stable isotope values (δ18O, δ2H in ‰)

steps.description: with precision better than ±0.2‰ and ±1‰ for δ 18 O and δ 2 H, respectively (Supplementary Dataset 3).
steps.searchable_summary: report analytical precision for stable isotope measurements
outputs: precision values (±0.2‰ for δ18O, ±1‰ for δ2H)
